content.text: Design change control is the process that governs modifications to the approved design after the handoff package has been frozen. For GenAI features, design changes are higher-stakes than for conventional software: a change to a refusal message affects safety behaviour; a change to the confidence display affects trust calibration; a change to the HITL banner affects regulatory compliance. Change control ensures these impacts are assessed before the change is implemented, not discovered in production.
Why Informal Changes Are Dangerous After Freeze

In conventional product development, a designer can update button copy or rearrange a layout during the sprint without triggering a formal review. In a GenAI feature, the same informal approach fails for three reasons:
: UI copy that mirrors the system prompt (e.g., suggested queries, slot hints) may now contradict the frozen prompt — causing confusing responses
: the Safety reviewer approved a specific refusal message and confidence display — changing either without re-review means the safety approval is based on a design that no longer exists
: the traceability matrix links wireframe nodes to eval test cases — a UI change that modifies a node's behaviour invalidates the test cases that cover it, silently removing test coverage

An informal design change after freeze is not 'just a small update' — it is an undocumented divergence between the approved design and the implemented design. The approved design is what Safety and Legal signed off on, not the new version.
Every post-freeze change request is classified into one of four categories before any work begins. The classification determines the approval path and whether the handoff package must be re-frozen.
Whitespace, font size, colour shade (not confidence indicator)
Tone of reprompt text, order of suggested queries, label wording for AI chip
New slot added, audience expanded, new AI capability in flow, model swapped
Product Owner + Engineering Lead + affected gate re-review
Refusal message text, confidence threshold, HITL banner, PII handling display
Gate 2 or Gate 3 re-review, depending on impact

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class ChangeCategory(Enum):
    COSMETIC           = "cosmetic"
    MINOR_BEHAVIORAL   = "minor_behavioral"
    SCOPE_CHANGE       = "scope_change"
    SAFETY_LEGAL       = "safety_legal"


@dataclass
class DesignChangeRequest:
    change_id: str
    description: str
    changed_component: str        # which UI component is affected
    changed_artifact_id: str      # BEH-SPEC-NN, NODE-XX, or PROMPT-vN
    reason: str
    urgency: str                  # "critical" | "high" | "normal"
    category: Optional[ChangeCategory] = None   # set by reviewer
    approved_by: str = ""
    approved_at: str = ""
    requires_gate_review: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # ["SAFETY", "LEGAL"]
    requires_refreeze: bool = False

    def classify(self) -> ChangeCategory:
        """
        Decision tree classification — called by the change control reviewer.
        In practice this is a human judgment call; this function shows the logic.
        """
        c = self.description.lower()
        safety_keywords = ["refusal", "confidence", "hitl", "pii", "guardrail",
                           "safety", "vulnerable", "escalat"]
        scope_keywords = ["new slot", "new capability", "audience", "model swap",
                          "scope", "expand"]

        if any(kw in c for kw in safety_keywords):
            return ChangeCategory.SAFETY_LEGAL
        if any(kw in c for kw in scope_keywords):
            return ChangeCategory.SCOPE_CHANGE
        if any(kw in c for kw in ["copy", "text", "wording", "label", "tone"]):
            return ChangeCategory.MINOR_BEHAVIORAL
        return ChangeCategory.COSMETIC

    def gate_reviews_required(self) -> list[str]:
        if self.category == ChangeCategory.SAFETY_LEGAL:
            desc = self.description.lower()
            gates = []
            if any(k in desc for k in ["refusal", "hitl", "confidence", "guardrail", "vulnerable"]):
                gates.append("SAFETY")
            if any(k in desc for k in ["pii", "data", "consent", "legal", "regulation"]):
                gates.append("LEGAL")
            return gates
        if self.category == ChangeCategory.SCOPE_CHANGE:
            return ["SAFETY", "LEGAL"]  # scope changes always trigger both
        return []
Every post-freeze design change follows a four-step workflow regardless of category. The only variable is who approves at step 3.
: the person requesting the change fills in the DesignChangeRequest fields — description, changed component, artifact ID, reason, urgency. The requester does not classify the change.

: the Design Lead reviews the request and assigns a ChangeCategory within one business day. If the classification is disputed, the Product Owner resolves it. Classification is a judgment call, not automated.

: cosmetic → no approval needed, log in PR; minor behavioural → Product Owner async approval; scope change → Product Owner + Engineering Lead + affected gate re-review (which may take 5–7 business days); safety/legal → affected gate(s) re-review

: once approved, the designer implements the change, updates the relevant artifact version, and the Design Lead triggers a handoff package re-freeze if required. The traceability matrix is updated to reference the new artifact version.
Every approved change that affects a versioned artifact must produce a new artifact version. The version bump is part of the change workflow, not optional.
Traceability row: update wireframe_node_id; eval test cases: update expected reprompt output
Gate 2 re-review; system prompt patch (if copy is in prompt); eval test case update
Gate 2 re-review; script prototype update with new low-confidence turn
All engineering tickets referencing old version updated; eval suite re-run against new version
Gates 1 and 2 re-review; full eval suite re-run; safety regression testing

When a safety-relevant defect is discovered in production — a refusal that is not triggering, a confidence badge showing wrong level, a HITL escalation not firing — the team needs to act faster than the normal change control timeline. The emergency protocol allows implementation before full re-approval, with an accelerated retrospective review.

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class EmergencyChangeRequest(DesignChangeRequest):
    """
    Used when a production safety defect requires an immediate fix.
    The fix can be deployed before gate re-review, but gate re-review
    is MANDATORY within 24 hours of deployment.
    """
    production_incident_id: str = ""
    deployed_at: str = ""        # timestamp of emergency deployment
    retrospective_review_due: str = ""  # must be within 24h of deployment
    retrospective_approved: bool = False

    def is_retrospective_overdue(self, now: str) -> bool:
        if not self.retrospective_review_due:
            return False
        return now > self.retrospective_review_due and not self.retrospective_approved

    def post_deployment_actions(self) -> list[str]:
        return [
            f"File gate re-review request within 4h of deployment ({self.production_incident_id})",
            f"Complete retrospective gate review by {self.retrospective_review_due}",
            "Update traceability matrix with emergency change record",
            "Run eval suite regression immediately after deployment",
            "Notify Safety Lead and Legal Lead of emergency change",
        ]

An emergency change that is not retrospectively approved within 24 hours must be reverted. There is no such thing as a permanent emergency exception to gate review — the emergency protocol shortens the timeline, it does not eliminate the requirement.

Every change to a frozen handoff package is audit evidence. For regulated features, the change log must be complete and irrefutable — if a change was made informally without a change request, it is as if the change was made without approval.

The handoff package has multiple consumers: engineering (implementing it), QA (testing against it), the safety reviewer (whose approval references specific versions), and legal (whose sign-off references specific data handling descriptions). When an artifact version changes, each consumer must be notified with enough context to know whether their work is affected.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable


CONSUMER_ROLES = {
    "engineering":  ["BEH-SPEC", "NODE", "HANDOFF", "PROMPT"],  # artifacts they implement
    "qa":           ["BEH-SPEC", "NODE", "SCRIPT", "EVAL"],      # artifacts they test against
    "safety":       ["BEH-SPEC", "PROMPT", "FMEA", "HITL"],      # artifacts safety approved
    "legal":        ["BEH-SPEC", "DATA-REQ", "COMPLIANCE"],      # artifacts legal approved
    "design":       ["NODE", "WIREFRAME", "SCRIPT"],              # artifacts designer owns
}


@dataclass
class ChangeNotification:
    change_id: str
    changed_artifact_id: str    # e.g. "BEH-SPEC-05-v3"
    changed_artifact_type: str  # "BEH-SPEC", "NODE", "PROMPT", etc.
    change_summary: str
    action_required: str        # what the consumer must do

    def consumers_to_notify(self) -> list[str]:
        return [
            role for role, artifacts in CONSUMER_ROLES.items()
            if any(self.changed_artifact_type.startswith(a) for a in artifacts)
        ]


def notify_consumers(
    notification: ChangeNotification,
    send_fn: Callable[[str, ChangeNotification], None],
) -> None:
    """
    send_fn(role, notification) — in practice, posts to a project management
    system channel or sends an email to the role's DL.
    """
    for role in notification.consumers_to_notify():
        send_fn(role, notification)


# Example: notify all consumers of a refusal policy update
refusal_change = ChangeNotification(
    change_id="CR-042",
    changed_artifact_id="BEH-SPEC-05-v3",
    changed_artifact_type="BEH-SPEC",
    change_summary="Refusal policy: added competitor product names to trigger list",
    action_required=(
        "Engineering: update system prompt to include new triggers. "
        "QA: re-run EVAL-RP-01 and EVAL-RP-02 against new prompt version. "
        "Safety: confirm new triggers do not introduce over-refusal pattern."
    ),
)
# consumers: ["engineering", "qa", "safety"]
Any change that bumps an artifact version requires a targeted regression test before the new version enters the next sprint. The regression scope depends on the change category.
Rerun eval cases that reference the changed artifact
Full eval suite for the affected flow + adjacent flows that share slots
Full eval suite + adversarial safety cases + regression against prior prompt version
Smoke test immediately post-deploy; full regression within 4h

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class RegressionTestPlan:
    change_id: str
    category: ChangeCategory
    eval_cases_to_run: list[str]   # eval test case IDs
    adjacent_flows: list[str]      # flow IDs that share state with the changed flow
    requires_adversarial_run: bool
    requires_prior_version_comparison: bool

    @classmethod
    def from_change(
        cls,
        change: DesignChangeRequest,
        traceability: "TraceabilityMatrix",
        adjacent_flow_ids: list[str],
    ) -> "RegressionTestPlan":
        if change.category == ChangeCategory.COSMETIC:
            return cls(
                change_id=change.change_id,
                category=change.category,
                eval_cases_to_run=[],
                adjacent_flows=[],
                requires_adversarial_run=False,
                requires_prior_version_comparison=False,
            )

        # Find all eval cases that reference the changed artifact
        affected_evals = [
            ec
            for row in traceability.rows
            if row.behavior_spec_id == change.changed_artifact_id
            or row.wireframe_node_id == change.changed_artifact_id
            for ec in row.eval_test_case_ids
        ]

        return cls(
            change_id=change.change_id,
            category=change.category,
            eval_cases_to_run=affected_evals,
            adjacent_flows=adjacent_flow_ids if change.category in (
                ChangeCategory.SCOPE_CHANGE, ChangeCategory.SAFETY_LEGAL
            ) else [],
            requires_adversarial_run=change.category == ChangeCategory.SAFETY_LEGAL,
            requires_prior_version_comparison=change.category == ChangeCategory.SAFETY_LEGAL,
        )

    def is_complete(
        self,
        ran_evals: set[str],
        adversarial_complete: bool,
    ) -> bool:
        evals_done = all(ec in ran_evals for ec in self.eval_cases_to_run)
        adversarial_done = (not self.requires_adversarial_run) or adversarial_complete
        return evals_done and adversarial_done

A change that does not pass its regression test must not be deployed, regardless of business urgency. If the urgency is genuine, use the emergency change protocol — which still requires a full regression within 4 hours of deployment.

Design change control after sign-off classifies every post-freeze modification into one of four categories — cosmetic (log only), minor behavioural (product owner approval), scope change (multi-gate re-review), or safety/legal relevant (gate re-review mandatory). Each category has a defined approval path, artifact version increment, and re-freeze requirement. Emergency changes are permitted for production safety defects but require retrospective gate review within 24 hours or must be reverted. Every change is logged as audit evidence — informal changes are indistinguishable from unauthorized changes in a regulatory audit.
: no approval, log in PR — colour, whitespace, purely visual adjustments
: product owner async approval, patch version bump on affected artifact
: product owner + engineering lead + affected gate re-review; new handoff version on completion
: gate 2 and/or gate 3 re-review; handoff package re-frozen only after re-approval
: deploy first, complete retrospective gate review within 24h, or revert
: every non-cosmetic change is logged with change_id, category, approver, and gate reviews — the complete audit trail for regulatory review
